Understand our road map for revolutionizing UX, how AI changes the idea of what an application is, and what all this means for you.

Thomas Reiss, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 11:50 am SUS1523

AI that helps you operate your business

As sustainability shifts from reporting to real business decisions, it becomes a control and execution challenge. Explore how you can replace manual processes with AI for trusted, scalable insights and decision support, delivering accountability for operational and financial decision-making.

Sophia Mendelsohn, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm PUX1195

Creating AI-powered UX for your own applications with SAP Design System

Learn how to create AI-powered UX for your applications by using SAP Design System (SAP Fiori UX for web and mobile apps) with its powerful set of reusable components, patterns, and floorplans. See how building applications is made easier with our AI-enabled UI technologies for web and mobile apps.

Sophia Levens, SAP

Frederic Berg, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm ERP2238

Western Sugar’s success with SAP Cloud ERP

Five years after go-live, Western Sugar Cooperative calls SAP Cloud ERP applications “wonderful.” Hear about why it chose public cloud; the value of clean core, best practices, strong configuration and integration, and continuous improvement; its smooth move from a two- to three-system landscape; and readiness for AI-enabled innovation.

 

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm FOU2337

When AI becomes the interface to the enterprise

For decades, SAP helped shape enterprise software based on screens and transactions. Today, AI is evolving how we interact with systems. Explore how SAP leads this shift—uniting applications, data, and AI—and what this next phase of transformation means for organizations turning AI into a real competitive advantage.

Florian Heretsch, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm FOU1557

Getting started with SAP Build MCPs: Create your first CAP app and process

 Experience the powerful Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration in SAP Build solutions. Produce your first SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application and SAP Fiori elements UI, while orchestrating workflows. See how AI-enhanced context improves coding efficiency and helps you build scalable, process-rich apps.

 

Tim Back, SAP

Jason Gabor, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm BTM1232

The AI-augmented architect: Upgrading architecture in the age of AI 

 AI is reshaping enterprise architecture. To provide value, enterprise architectures must embrace AI tools. Discover SAP technology leveraging AI for enhanced visibility and coordination. Through real-world examples, see how Joule and agentic AI increase information velocity and augment architect capabilities to thrive in this evolving landscape.

Martin Mysyk, SAP

Wednesday
1:30 pm – 1:50 pm PUX1192

From enterprise software to AI native experiences: SAP's UX strategy
As AI reshapes the enterprise, SAP's design strategy puts human intent at the center of autonomous, agentic, and multimodal Joule solution experiences, turning complexity into clarity and technology into measurable business value. Take a look at where user engagement and enterprise UX are headed, and how SAP is leading the way.

Arin Bhowmick, SAP

Sophia Levens, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm PUX1210

AI and the future of user experience in SAP software

Prepare yourself for the upcoming revolution in user experience by understanding how we are harnessing AI to help users achieve better outcomes. Discover how AI is changing the idea of what an application is—and what this means for you. Learn about our AI design patterns and get a glimpse of the future SAP software user experience.

Emil Voutta, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm JOU2647

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

The enterprise application is no longer a system of record but a system of action. As AI moves from assistant to agent, organizations shift from what their software can store to what it can do. Learn why the future belongs to applications that don't just surface information but understand context, anticipate decisions, and act with confidence.

 

Wednesday
3:00 pm – 3:20 pm PUX1194

User experience innovations and road map for SAP Business Suite

Have a look at the latest user experience innovations across our suite of applications, along with exciting web and mobile innovations coming soon. Also check out selected highlights from Ul technologies for creating great UX for your own applications.

Tom Reiss, SAP

Christina Salwitzek, SAP

I’m excited to hear what we announce in the keynotes and experience the future of enterprise software. AI will transform how we all interact with apps. The future starts now.

Here are some other summaries of Sapphire sessions from other colleagues.

Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development by Mark Wright

See you in Orlando!

For the SAP Cloud ERP UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: SAP Developer News for April 16th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS

UX Innovation Day in Silicon Valley on 11 June 

  • Event page https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html  

SAP BTP ABAP Environment – Pre-Upgrade Option for Release 2605 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-btp-abap-environment-pre-upgrade-option-for-release-2605/ba-p/14347147  

XML Annotations: The Essentials Before Adapting Documentation Samples to Your Project 

  • Mio’s blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/xml-annotations-the-essentials-before-adapting-documentation-samples-to/ba-p/14369241 
  • OData Deep Dive mission https://developers.sap.com/mission.odata-deep-dive.html  
  • OData Deep Dive rewrite blog post https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/02/02/odata-deep-dive-rewrite-in-the-open/  

Edge Integration Cell APIs and Cloud Integration AI-assisted error resolution 

  • Edge Integration Cell APIs: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/local-odata-api-access-for-edge-integration-cell?locale=en-US  
  • AI-assisted error resolution: https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/analyze-message-processing-errors-with-ai-f27f95d7b90b4f13aea22b399418b702?locale=en-US  

DYK #12 AI Launchpad Leaderboard 

  • The Model Library topic in the SAP Help Portal https://help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad/sap-ai-launchpad/model-library  

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro 

00:07 UX Innovation Day in Silicon Valley on 11 June 

01:00 SAP BTP ABAP Environment – Pre-Upgrade Option for Release 2605 

02:00 XML Annotations: The Essentials Before Adapting Documentation Samples to Your Project 

03:16 Edge Integration Cell APIs and Cloud Integration AI-assisted error resolution 

04:25 DYK #12 AI Launchpad Leaderboard 

TRANSCRIPT

[INTRO]
This is the SAP Developer News for April the 16th, 2026.

[DJ]
If you're in the Silicon Valley area in June and are interested in the intersection between AI and UX, then there's an event just for you. It's on the 11th of June at the SAP AppHaus in Palo Alto, and it's an in-person event only. It's for developers, architects, and technical consultants, and it's all about the AI functionality being built into the SAP Fiori elements framework. There's AI for business users and AI for application developers. The event page has an agenda for the day, which is currently tentative, but it gives us a good idea of the format and content. There's also a link on there for registration. Anyway, check it out. Link in the description.

[Mamikee]
We have an important update for SAP BTP ABAP environment users. We're offering a free pre-upgrade option for release 2605, giving customers and partners the chance to test their existing applications ahead of the standard upgrade, which is planned for May 16th and 17th. Now some of the key milestones have already passed. Planned new features were announced on April April 13th, and pre-upgraded systems are scheduled for upgrade on April 17th, which means teams already participating should now be focused on regression testing and validating their custom-built apps. Now, looking ahead, Hot Fix Collection 2 is planned for April 30th, and any issues found during testing should be reported by May 1st using component BC-CP-ABA. Now, the goal is simple. help make the upgrade a no-event and avoid day one surprises. You can read more in the link below.

[DJ]
Fiori elements based UIs are great, but sometimes it's hard for us to grok how they can be controlled and configured, which is mostly through annotations, which are organized in vocabularies, expressed in metadata, which is usually in the form of XML. So any help is welcome. And in that context, we wanted to point you to a great blog post by SAP community member Mio Yasutake, who takes time to explain these topics in great detail, how things fit together based upon samples and documentation for UI5 and in the context of CAP and RAP-based services. There's lots of detail and it's well worth a read. Thanks Mio! Also if you want more detail to complement this then we have the OData deep dive mission over on the tutorial navigator and in there there are specific tutorials on metadata vocabularies and annotations ready to go as well. Happy learning!

[Antonio]
Hola, SAP Developers! Two quick updates for Edge Integration Cell and Cloud Integration users. First, the local OData APIs for Edge Integration Cell just got better. You can now authenticate using Client ID and Client Secret. More secure, easy to automate. Now, a quick reminder that there are many APIs in Edge Integration Cell that you can use. Monitoring APIs, for example, to track message processing. and management APIs for message stores, data stores, JMS brokers, and variables. These are perfect for building custom dashboards or for automating operations or integrating with your existing monitoring tools. Now, in Cloud Integration, we have AI-assisted error resolution. When your integration flow fails, you can now use AI to analyze the error, get root cause suggestions, and even get remediation steps. So it's like having an integration buddy next to you. which can save time debugging and gets you back on track faster. So check the links in the description for full documentation on both features. ¡Hasta luego!

[Nora]
Did you know that you can compare foundation models right in AI Launchpad? So just jump over to Generative AI Hub in your AI Launchpad and open the model library. And then here you can click on Leaderboard. And now you see all the models that we have available here. and over here you can see the different scores so the HELM score for example tells you how well the model performed in a multiple choice exam across a variety of topics the chatbot arena score tells you how well the model performed in a head-to -head against different AI models and then the AIRBench refusal rate and the AIRBench discrimination bias refusal rate tells you how well the model is handling harmful or biased requests and then all the way over here you have the output and input token cost and the context window size. Now this really helped me to find the right model for my use case depending on cost and context window size and performance overall. I honestly usually don't go to the leaderboard, I head directly to the chart because here you can very easily compare the models.

entry.title: Step-by-Step Integration of SAP Cloud ALM with S/4HANA for Transport Management
entry.content:
This blog outlines the process of integrating SAP Cloud ALM with SAP S/4 HANA to enable efficient transport management. The integration ensures streamlined lifecycle management, improved visibility, and better control over transport activities across the SAP landscape.

The overall process involves the following key steps:

  1. Onboarding SAP Cloud ALM

Before initiating the integration, SAP Cloud ALM must be provisioned and onboarded in your SAP environment.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following requirement is met before starting the onboarding process:

You must have an S-user ID with the “Edit Cloud Data” authorization in SAP for Me.

Procedure

  • Log in to SAP for Me.
  • Navigate to Systems & Provisioning.
  • Under Products Available for Provisioning, locate your entitlement for SAP Cloud ALM.

  • Choose Start Provisioning.

Select the geographical region in which you would like to provision SAP Cloud ALM 

  • Enter a unique subdomain name.
  • Specify an SAP Cloud Identity Service, if you have an existing one please select it from the list, if you do not have an existing SAP Cloud Identity Service a new one will be created for you automatically.

  • After selecting Submit, the provisioning process is initiated. SAP Cloud ALM is automatically prepared by SAP, and the required foundational entities are created to enable the service.

During this process, the following components are provisioned:

  • A SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) Global Account and Subaccount dedicated to SAP Cloud ALM are created. Within this subaccount, you will generate the Service API key, which is required to connect SAP Cloud ALM with other systems in your landscape.
  • A mutual trust relationship is automatically established between SAP Cloud ALM and the selected SAP Cloud Identity Authentication Service tenant to enable secure authentication and user access.

Once SAP completes the setup of these entities, you will receive a confirmation email containing the URL to access your newly provisioned SAP Cloud ALM tenant.

  1. System Prerequisites

SAP_BASIS Release

Your ABAP system must meet one of the following requirements:

  • SAP_BASIS 7.40 SP16 or higher (or 7.50 SP05 or higher)
  • SAP_BASIS 7.40 SP09 – SP15 (or 7.50 SP00 – SP04) with implementation of SAP Note 2283880

SAP_UI Version

The minimum required version is:

  • SAP_UI 740 SP15 or higher

ST-PI Component

Ensure the ST-PI component is properly maintained:

  • Install ST-PI 7.40 support packages and keep them up to date.
  • Implement the required collective correction SAP Note 3639977, which includes updates for SAP Cloud ALM compatibility.
  • Always operate the ABAP system with the latest or second latest support package available on the SAP Support Portal.

ST-A/PI Version

For Business Process Monitoring in SAP S/4HANA or SAP Business Suite 7:

  • Use ST-A/PI version higher than 01U_731.
  • Keep the component updated regularly.
  • If using ST-A/PI version 01W, ensure that the latest related SAP Notes are implemented.

Security and SSL Configuration

The following profile parameters and certificates must be configured in the ABAP system:

Required Profile Parameters

  • icm/HTTPS/client_sni_enabled = TRUE
  • ssl/client_ciphersuites must be maintained according to SAP Note 510007

Required Root Certificates

The following certificates must be imported in STRUST under:

  • SSL Client (Anonymous)
  • SSL Client (Standard)

Certificates required:

  • DigiCert Global Root G2
  • DigiCert TLS RSA4096 Root G5

Required User Authorizations

The user who runs transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP must be assigned the following PFCG role:

  • SAP_SDF_ALM_SETUP

Within this role, maintain the authorization object:

  • S_BTCH_NAM → BTCUNAME

The value for BTCUNAME can be maintained as:

  • * (asterisk) to allow any user to execute the background job, or
  • The specific username that will run the background job for data collection.

Background Job User designated to run the data collection background jobs must have the appropriate roles and authorizations required for SAP Cloud ALM data integration. 

Configure Transport Management System (STMS) in SAP S/4HANA

Log in to the SAP S/4HANA application server and configure the SAP Transport Management System (STMS) to enable transport management across your system landscape.

In this example, a two-system landscape is used:

  • Development System: NI2
  • Production System: PI2

The first step is to establish the STMS configuration between the NI2 (Development) system and the PI2 (Production) system. This configuration ensures that transport requests created in the development system can be properly routed and imported into the production system following the defined transport path.

Make sure CTC parameter value maintained in DEV and prod system as 1

  1. Connecting the On-Premise System to SAP Cloud ALM

Once the SAP Transport Management System (STMS) configuration is completed between the systems, the next step is to integrate the on-premise SAP S/4HANA system with SAP Cloud ALM.

This integration enables SAP Cloud ALM to monitor transport activities, manage deployment processes, and provide centralized visibility across the entire system landscape. 

Integration Setup Across System Landscapes

To establish the integration with SAP Cloud ALM, the required configuration steps must be performed in both the Development (DEV) and Production (PROD) systems. In addition, the setup should be executed in client 000 as well as in the productive client of each system.

While the technical configuration steps remain largely the same across environments, the purpose and use cases of each client and system differ:

  • Client 000 – Typically used for system-level configuration and technical setup activities.
  • Productive Client – Used for business operations and application-related processes.
  • Development (DEV) System – Used for development, configuration, and testing activities.
  • Production (PROD) System – Used for live business operations.

By ensuring that the integration is properly configured across these systems and clients, SAP Cloud ALM can effectively monitor system activities, manage transports, and support lifecycle operations across the entire SAP landscape. 

Login to 000 in client /n/SDF/ALM_SETUP

Maintaining the HTTP Destination

To configure the HTTP destination, a service key from SAP Cloud ALM is required. This service key contains the necessary credentials and endpoint details that allow the on-premise system to securely communicate with the cloud environment.

The service key is generated within SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), where SAP Cloud ALM is provisioned.

Procedure

  1. Log in to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) cockpit.
  2. Navigate to the subaccount associated with your SAP Cloud ALM tenant.
  3. Locate the SAP Cloud ALM service instance.
  4. Generate a service key, which will be used to maintain the HTTP destination in the on-premise system.

The information from the service key will later be used to configure the required communication settings between the on-premise SAP S/4HANA system and SAP Cloud ALM.

Registering the System in SAP Cloud ALM

Enter the background user that was created to perform the data collection tasks. Ensure that this user has the required authorizations as described in the Prerequisites section.

Next, choose Register to initiate the connection with SAP Cloud ALM and register the system. If the registration is successful, an LMS ID from the Landscape Management Service is automatically retrieved and displayed. This ID confirms that the system has been successfully registered in SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. Activating Transport Management Use Cases in SAP Cloud ALM

After registering the system using transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP, the next step is to activate the required transport-related use cases in SAP Cloud ALM.
The tasks to be activated depend on the system landscape (DEV, QUA, PRD) and the client in which the configuration is performed.

  1. Development System (DEV)

Client 000: Activate the following task:

Transports: Read; This task must be activated on source systems to report transport requests to SAP Cloud ALM so that they can be assigned to features.

Client 100 (Development Productive Client)

Activate: Transports: Create & Export (Client-Specific)

This task must be activated on all development clients used for customizing activities.

For example:

  • If customizing activities are performed in client 100, activate the task there.
  • If another client such as 200 is used for development or transport creation, the task must also be activated in that client.

Activating this task triggers the background job:

/SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_PROC_CL_DEP-XXX

This enables the following transport-related functions:

  • Create transports
  • Create transport of copies
  • Release transports
  • Delete empty transports during release

Transports: Retrofit (Client-Specific)

Activate this task in the development system of your maintenance track if retrofit functionality is required.

Retrofit ensures that changes implemented in one landscape can be synchronized with another development track.

  1. Quality System (QUA)

Client 000; Activate the following task:

Transports: Import

This triggers the background job:

/SDF/CALM_CDM_IMPORT_TRANSPORTS

The job performs the following actions:

  • Retrieves transports scheduled for import from SAP Cloud ALM
  • Executes the transport import process into the target system

Target systems typically include:

  • Quality Assurance (QA)
  • Pre-Production
  • Production

These systems are assigned to the project via the deployment plan.

  1. Production System (PRD)

Client 000; Activate the following tasks:

Transports: Import

Allows SAP Cloud ALM to trigger transport imports into the production system.

Transports: Read Landscape

This task reports the STMS landscape configuration to SAP Cloud ALM.

Supported landscape configurations include:

  • Any consistent Transport Management System (TMS) landscape
  • The last system in a transport track is always considered the production system
  • Client-specific transport routes (CTC) are recommended
  • TMS transport groups are supported

Transports: Check; This feature enables transport impact analysis before importing transports into production.

It evaluates the transport set to identify potential risks or conflicts that may impact the production environment.

Do the same for 000 and productive client of production system as well.

  1. Background Jobs Created After SAP Cloud ALM Configuration

Once the configuration and system registration are completed in SAP Cloud ALM, several background jobs are automatically scheduled in the SAP S/4HANA (or ABAP) system. These jobs are responsible for data collection, system monitoring, transport processing, and communication with SAP Cloud ALM.

Some of the commonly scheduled jobs include:

  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_DIAGNOSTICS_000
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_DIAGNOSTICS_<Productive Client>
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_CHECK
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_PROC_CL_DEP-<Client No.>

In addition, the following operational jobs may also be scheduled:

  • CALM Auto Discovery – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_00
  • CALM Heartbeat – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_000
  • CALM Scheduler – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_000

These jobs ensure continuous communication, monitoring, and transport management synchronization between the on-premise system and SAP Cloud ALM.

Now go to projects and setup and add the system.

Add the systems to SAP Cloud ALM based on the number of systems available in your landscape. In this example, the environment consists of two landscapes: Development and Production. These systems are added and organized accordingly to enable proper monitoring, transport management, and lifecycle operations within SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. Creating Deployment Plans

The next step is to create deployment plans in SAP Cloud ALM. Deployment plans define how transports move across the system landscape and support different change management scenarios.

These plans may vary depending on organizational requirements and client-specific processes.

Common examples include:

  • Normal Change
  • Standard Change etc.

Create the required deployment plans based on your organization’s change management process. After creating the plans, assign the system groups that were configured in the earlier steps. This ensures that transports are deployed according to the defined system landscape and transport routes.

By configuring deployment plans properly, SAP Cloud ALM can effectively manage and control the movement of transports across development, testing, and production environments.

In the next step, assign the required roles for Change and Deployment Management in SAP Cloud ALM. These roles define the responsibilities of users involved in the change management process.

Typical roles include Project Lead, Change Manager, Developer, and other relevant stakeholders. Assigning these roles ensures proper governance, clear accountability, and smooth execution of the change and transport management process.

With these steps completed, the configuration and integration with SAP Cloud ALM are successfully finalized. The on-premise systems are now connected, enabling centralized monitoring, transport management, and change and deployment management across the system landscape

  1. System Refresh Considerations

After connecting your ABAP system to SAP Cloud ALM, the system continuously sends monitoring data to the SAP Cloud ALM monitoring applications.

The monitoring data is associated with a specific ABAP system in the Landscape Management application based on the Landscape Management System (LMS) ID.

When a system refresh is performed—where an ABAP system is overwritten with a copy of another system—the original LMS ID mapping may become inconsistent. As a result, monitoring data may incorrectly be assigned to the source system of the copy rather than to the refreshed system.

To prevent this issue, it is recommended to save and restore the tables containing SAP Cloud ALM monitoring configuration during the refresh process.

Post-Copy Automation (PCA) Integration

The SAP Cloud ALM configuration is integrated with Post-Copy Automation (PCA) to simplify system copy and refresh scenarios.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following components are available:

  • ST-PI 7.40 SP27 or higher
  • Implementation of SAP Note 3501074 (System Copy: ST-PI CALM Configuration)

Reports for System Copy and Refresh

If Post-Copy Automation (PCA) is not used, SAP provides dedicated reports to support system copy and refresh scenarios related to SAP Cloud ALM configuration.

Report: /SDF/CALM_SYS_COPY

This report is used after performing a system copy to create a new system based on an already configured ABAP system.

The report deletes the existing SAP Cloud ALM configuration associated with the copied system to prevent incorrect system mapping.

After running this report, the connection to SAP Cloud ALM must be re-established using transaction:

/SDF/ALM_SETUP

To prepare a system refresh, you can execute this report. It creates a Transport of Copies (ToC) with the table content of the SAP Cloud ALM configuration. Export this request and save it. After the system refresh, you can import this transport request again.

entry.title: SAP User Feedback Days 2026: Two days of listening, learning, & building real connection in Walldorf
entry.content:
On April 15 and 16, 2026, we welcomed end users and experts from our customers to SAP Headquarters in Walldorf for a two-day event built around one simple idea: We Create the Future. We can say with absolute certainty that the event was a huge success—and what made it special wasn’t just the agenda, the sessions, or the logistics. It was the people. The honesty. The energy. The shared curiosity. And the feeling—again and again—that when we sit together and talk openly, we move faster and we build better. It is a source of joy and pride for us to host this event at SAP.

What SAP User Feedback Days are really about:

  • To create a space where customers feel truly heard
  • To bring product teams and user research together with end users’ face to face
  • To dig deeply into real use cases and the reality of daily work
  • To challenge our assumptions (and sometimes our favorite ideas) in a constructive way
  • To build relationships that last beyond the workshops

Over the two days, we heard a consistent theme from customers: they value not only the chance to influence the future of SAP software, but also the human closeness—the ability to meet the people behind the product, and to meet other experts from other companies who “get it”.

Day 1: Deep dives with real end users—where insights become tangible

The first day was dedicated to hands-on user research and direct exchange on specific AI use cases coming from our product teams. Our user research experts worked side-by-side with customers and our product teams to explore real workflows, pain points, decision moments, and what “good” looks like in practice.

One of the most meaningful things we heard was that some customers said they didn’t realize SAP was truly listening in this way. This is so motivating for us all and to see this in action together —it’s exactly why these days matter. Trust is built in moments like these.

   

Kicked off with a keynote on "We Create the Future".

 

Intense, but very fun, sessions with our customers on AI use cases!

Day 2: Looking forward—co-creating what comes next

If day one was about listening deeply to current reality, day two was about looking forward together. Through interactive workshops and collaborative discussions, customers and SAP colleagues explored what the future should feel like: where user experience must evolve, what users need from SAP to succeed, and how we can design for the next set of challenges coming in business and from AI technology —not in isolation, but together.

 

Our User Research Event lead, Christoph, driving a session with customers.

   

One of our expert sessions on Fast with Fiori.

 

Expert sessions and interactive exchanges: making it easy to connect beyond “the session”

In addition to the research activities, customers also had the chance to join expert sessions and explore interactive topics, designed to give space for informal questions, deeper product conversations, and spontaneous discussions.

Many customers told us they were impressed—not only by what SAP is working on, but by how approachable the experts were. These exchanges helped customers see the “why” behind decisions, and helped our teams see where clarity, guidance, and experience design need to be even stronger.

It was also a pleasure to experience our product teams learn more about what is so important about their products and use cases and also sometimes challenge their assumptions and see a different way forward. Plus, it was amazing to see them take time to show our customers our wonderful campus and explain why we like working so much for SAP.

Why this matters: better UX starts with closeness, not distance

We often talk about “customer centricity.” SAP User Feedback Days are what it looks like when we practice it.

Thank you—truly

To our customers and end users: thank you for making the trip, for trusting us with your honest feedback, and for being willing to engage so openly. The event would not be what it is without you—and it genuinely wouldn’t be the same without your presence and energy.

To the SAP colleagues across user research, product teams, and all the experts who supported sessions and exchanges: thank you for the preparation, professionalism, and care you brought into every customer interaction. The way you showed up made a real difference to how customers experienced SAP.

We’re already looking forward to what comes next—and to continuing the conversation, not only at the next event, but in the everyday work between events.

Customer Voices

Just a few comments that I can share from the day from our customers

  • “I brought my business colleague with me, to show him as well…..to see his excitement when.. he was working through the app, it was very delightful for me.”
  • “SAP is really going into the AI direction, that can help us automate our processes, which is great for us.” 
  • “Whoever, might be invited in future, I will just recommend to.. use this opportunity to.. come into this exchange. …. it's a great thing.” 
  • “Thanks to SAP for the invitation and the open exchange — this is exactly the kind of practical AI innovation our industry needs.”
  • Want to see some more proof? Check out the LinkedIn buzz here!!!

 

What’s next

We’re now consolidating insights and findings from the two days and working with the teams involved to ensure what we learned translates into tangible improvements—both in the near term and in future product direction.

If you joined us in Walldorf: thank you again. And please keep the conversation going—we truly value it!

From a very happy Alex & Walli

And not forgetting thank you from our project dream team!

entry.title: Celebrating my 300th blog post on the SAP Community
entry.content:
I noticed that my last blog post was my 300th on the SAP Community. In those 300 posts, I have covered many topics related to user experience, clustered into a few key areas. To celebrate this milestone, I’ll reflect on how the topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP, what I learned from the community, what’s next, and end with a call to action for you.

My 300 blogs posts contain a historical account on the evolution of SAP’s user experience

Product Marketing is much easier with great products

Before going into the content, let me acknowledge that there is no product marketing without product. All these blog posts about our UX products would not have happened without the amazing development, product management, design, QA, documentation, and other colleagues that build and test these solutions. I’m grateful to the many coworkers over the years who came up with ideas to solve real customer pain points regarding SAP’s user experience. I also appreciate the many customers and partners that provided ideas for blogs posts in online forums, SAP events, and user groups.

The topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP

In my product marketing role, most of my posts fell into one of these broad categories:

  • Product
  • Strategy
  • Events
  • Customer and partner enablement

The topic mix shifted over time as our team expanded the products we worked on.

Product

  • Release announcements.
    Product-focused posts generally followed the cadence of our release cycles. This shifted from annual or semi-annual service packs for on-prem solutions to continuous innovation / continuous delivery pipelines for our cloud-based tools. My first five years of posts were on SAP Screen Personas. From version 1.0’s first appearance at TechEd 2012 through the release announcements of version 3 SP18, after which Rohith Deraje took over for SP19 and beyond.
    SAP Fiori elements and SAP Fiori tools were next to join our team’s portfolio. I posted some of the early announcements for those products until Katrin Polloczek, Christoph Gollmick, Ashley Tung, and Ian Quigley took ownership of the content.
    Collaborative ERP was a more recent addition to SAP UX Foundation. I posted the early announcements on our strategy and early versions until Regina Sheynblat picked this up.
    Embedded AI in SAP Fiori apps is the newest part of our portfolio, with Conrad Bernal now posting about new innovations and beta opportunities.
  • Roadmap updates – when you have a product that 1000s of customers use to run their business, they want to know what’s coming next. Towards that end, I drafted many roadmap updates over the years on the various solutions in our portfolio.
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQ) documents – one of the most interesting parts of my role is interacting with customers in various forums and hearing their questions. Often, we hear the same questions from multiple people. In those cases, it’s worth capturing them into an FAQ document.
  • Technical tips and tricks – as part of the product team, many customers reach out vie email to ask questions that are more detailed than can be covered in an FAQ document. If their inquiry is relevant to a broader audience, I put together tips they can use to get started on a project or break through a barrier they are facing and post it so others in the SAP Community can learn.
  • Customer and partner success stories – sometimes our customers want to share their success with SAP UX solutions. In those cases, I have helped them tell their story through the SAP Community.

Strategy

When I looked through my blog posts over time, they followed the same evolution of SAP’s broader UX strategy. We built SAP Screen Personas to improve the SAP GUI user experience, one screen at a time. SAP Fiori elements accomplished this at scale, for the most common business processes. Then we rolled out SAP Fiori tools to make it easier to build Fiori elements apps. Then collaborative ERP to help people team up to solve complex business problems. Most recently, we added AI to both the Fiori apps that we ship and the tools we (and you) use to build them.

Posts on UX strategy explain how all the pieces fit together so customers can plan their digital transformation, SAP S/4HANA migration, or other strategic initiative knowing where SAP is heading. Since customers are not concerned about our internal organizational structures, I blogged on solutions within the UX Foundation portfolio as well as adjacent solutions such as SAP Build, BTP, and Joule. Sometimes we include a peek behind the curtain, so people understand how SAP Fiori powers the apps in SAP S/4HANA and what to expect as we continue to add both simplicity and power to our flagship SAP Cloud ERP solution.

Events

It’s natural to blog about events to help people set the right expectations, plan their agendas, and make the most of their investment in attending the event. Typically, I like to highlight the sessions most relevant to the customers and partners that follow me on the SAP Community and LinkedIn.

For Sapphire 2026, I have a list of recommended sessions on AI and UX, along with what I’m planning with Nicole Smythe on bridging the skills gap to accelerate your AI projects.

My other posts have covered SAP TechEd, ASUG Tech Connect, ASUG chapter meetings, and, of course, our own SAP UX Innovation Day events. Next one is on June 11 in Palo Alto, if you are interested in attending.

Customer and partner enablement

I also noticed a number of blog posts on various training courses for customers, partners, and colleagues. In the early days, we created several openSAP training courses. The ones that are still relevant have moved to learning.sap.com. We also have posts on hands-on exercises from other events or tutorials from our development experts. It makes sense that people appreciate training from the team that builds and supports the products.

The SAP community is for learning, connecting, and listening

I would be remiss if I did not reflect on the SAP Community and the role it played in my personal growth at SAP. I learned so much from the questions and comments that people submitted, and through my interactions with the highly professional SAP Community team.

Much more than the number of blog posts I have written, I’m most proud of how many other colleagues I inspired to start blogging; that list continues to grow.

And thanks to my long-time friend, former colleague, and co-author, Michael Procopio, for getting me started on my blogging journey back in our days at HP Software.

The next UI will build from our Fiori experience and use AI heavily

While SAP Fiori solves many issues, we are still working to simplify how customers consume SAP and developers create apps. Needless to say, AI will play an increasingly important role in both of these dimensions. Your ongoing feedback will continue to drive our development priorities.
You will hear more about this at Sapphire and in our next SAP UX Innovation Day.

In addition to providing your feedback through the SAP Community, you are welcome to join our monthly SAP Developer Roundtable to share your thoughts on developing SAP Fiori apps using our latest tools and technologies.
If you are a public cloud customer, you can join our online user group to discuss UX and adjacent topics with your peers and SAP experts.

 

We will continue to address your concerns about usability in SAP Cloud ERP.

To count to 300, start with 1: What are you waiting for?

A much bigger milestone than blog 300 is blog #1.
What are you waiting for? There’s no time like the present!
The SAP Community needs your voice. If you want to brainstorm ideas or need help outlining your first post, please let me know. Like the rest of the SAP Community team, I’m here to help.

Thanks for reading all my stuff over the years.

entry.title: Turning “Moments of truth” into lasting impressions with SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding
entry.content:
"Moments of truth" in Onboarding

Admittedly, it has been a while since I studied Service Management at business school, and many of the theories date back to the 1980s. Still, one idea has always stayed with me: people judge experiences by comparing their expectations with their perception. In short, this is how we assess an experience. These are often referred to as “Moments of Truth.”

Even today, this concept remains highly relevant, if you ask me.

In the employee journey, one of the most important moments (of truth) happens after the contract is signed, when preboarding and onboarding begin. This is where expectations are tested, and first impressions are formed through communication, preparation, and early interactions.

It is both emotional and practical, and these first impressions tend to last.

Across industries, a common pattern is that organizations invest heavily in attracting talent, but far less in structuring the onboarding experience from “offer signed” to day one. People and relationships remain essential, but without structure and process support, the experience often becomes inconsistent.

This is where SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding can support organizations by enabling a more consistent, structured, and scalable onboarding experience. If it is used to its full extend.

 

From candidate to employee - without friction

At its core, SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding connects what is typically disconnected. It brings together systems, processes, and people into one experience.

Instead of handing a new hire over from recruiting into emails, PDFs, and spreadsheets, everything continues in one flow. The candidate becomes a new hire, and then an employee, without changing platform or experience.

The solution supports onboarding, cross-boarding, offboarding, and rehire scenarios in one place. This reflects the reality that employee journeys are not always linear.

In many implementations, the value of having one centralized experience is initially underestimated. In practice, it significantly reduces friction and manual coordination.

 

A guided, structured onboarding experience that sets people up for success

The system is designed around guided processes. New hires are not expected to figure things out themselves. Instead, they are supported step by step through tasks such as data collection, document handling, learning, and introductions.


 

Before day one, employees can access a personalized homepage to complete forms and prepare. On day one, the focus shifts to connections, clarity, and productivity. Activities such as buddy assignments, meetings, and onboarding tasks are already planned.

This creates a more consistent onboarding experience while still allowing flexibility for different roles and local requirements.

From a process perspective, this reduces waiting time and helps employees become productive earlier.

 

Seamless onboarding and data flow across the SuccessFactors suite

One of the most powerful aspects of SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding is its native connection to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central.

This is not a traditional integration. Data entered during onboarding is written directly into Employee Central. There is no duplication, no interfaces, and no need to re-enter information. From day one, you have one consistent and reliable data foundation.

What makes this particularly strong is that it applies to all actors involved in the process. The new hire, even before officially starting and while still an external user, can access a secure part of the system and enter their own data directly into the core HR system. The same applies to hiring managers and HR, who can update or enrich data during onboarding. Everything is written to the same place, in real time.

This has a clear practical impact. It reduces errors, removes manual steps, and ensures that employee data is available immediately for downstream processes.

Building on this foundation, onboarding also connects to the broader HR landscape. Recruiting triggers onboarding, Learning can assign training, and Performance can support early goal setting.

In many organizations, these processes are still handled separately, and expectations for the first days are often loosely defined and depend on individual managers.

By linking onboarding to structured plans such as 30-60-90 day goals, organizations can define expectations early, assign learning before day one, and track progress from the start.

This is where onboarding moves beyond administration and becomes part of broader workforce enablement.

 

Bringing structure and control to onboarding

From an HR and manager perspective, the value is not only in the experience, but also in the control.

Dashboards provide visibility into tasks, deadlines, and overdue activities. Tasks can be triggered automatically, reminders can be sent, and bottlenecks can be identified early.



What was previously handled through manual follow-ups becomes structured workflows.

This is particularly relevant in multi-country environments, where consistency and compliance are important.

The system allows configuration of business rules, automation of processes, and integration with the broader HR landscape through APIs. This means onboarding becomes part of the overall HR architecture rather than a standalone process.

 

What value does this actually create?

If we move beyond system terminology, the value can be summarized in a few key outcomes:

  • New hires arrive better prepared and ready to contribute from day one
  • Managers gain clarity on responsibilities and timelines
  • HR achieves greater consistency and visibility across teams and locations
  • Time to productivity is reduced through better preparation and alignment
  • Onboarding becomes structured and measurable rather than ad hoc

And importantly:

  • Organizations improve their ability to deliver a consistent first impression

Because onboarding is not just an HR activity. It is a key moment where expectations are either confirmed or challenged.



Closing thoughts

Many organizations focus heavily on attracting talent, but less on what happens immediately after.

If onboarding still relies on emails, documents, and manual follow-ups, it may be worth reviewing how the process is structured.

Improving onboarding is not only about efficiency. It is about shaping the employee experience at a critical moment.

 

Jonas Kyhnau is a SAP SuccessFactors Senior Consultant working at Pentos Denmark.
I help companies optimising and streamlining their core HR processes with practical, scalable solutions. I cover all process areas from Recruiting (including SmartRecruiters of course) through Onboarding to Core HR in Employee Central.

SAP SuccessFactors Platform SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding

#Pentos - SAP SuccessFactors Partner Danmark

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - What’s New in Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment [V3.0]
entry.content:
SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – What’s New in the 2026 Update of Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment

As you may already know, SAP continues to recommend deploying SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA using an embedded SAP Fiori Front-End Server.

Over the years, many customers who initially started with a standalone FES have been transitioning to embedded deployment to reduce complexity, optimise operations, and align with SAP’s long term architecture.
Looking ahead, this direction becomes even more explicit. As outlined in the SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations  (as of Guide’s Version 10.3, February 2026) under the section Outlook: Fiori Deployment, the guide explicitly states:

SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations, Section 'Outlook: Fiori Deployment''

“Please be aware that as of SAP S/4HANA 2027 the embedded deployment option will be the only available deployment option”. This effectively makes embedded deployment not just the recommended approach, but the standard going forward for SAP Fiori in SAP S/4HANA landscapes.

In this context, we’re excited to share the updated “Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA”  guide, based on SAP S/4HANA 2025, which becomes even more relevant for customers planning or progressing through this transition.

The guide was first published in October 2019 (version 1.0). It has since evolved through multiple updates, including version 2.1 in December 2024, which focused on SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2023. The most recent version, 3.0 released in March 2026, is based on SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2025.

Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA (V3.0)

 This latest update (Version 3.0, March 2026) has been driven with strong input from SAP product experts, including Peter Persiel (peterpersiel) , whose contributions have helped shape the guide based on real-world customer experiences and evolving SAP Fiori architecture.

So what actually changed?

This blog highlights the latest updates and key enhancements introduced in the 2026 version of the guide (Version 3.0 March 2026), helping you quickly understand what’s new, what’s improved, and what to consider for your projects going forward.

Why this update matters?

Version 3.0 of this guide is an important refresh. It reflects:

  • Alignment with SAP S/4HANA 2025 and SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2025, and important announcements planned in the future releases of SAP S/4HANA.
  • The continued evolution of SAP Fiori architecture and activation tooling, especially in embedded deployments
  • Lessons learned from more recent customer implementations, translating real project experience into more practical guidance
  • Increased focus on clean core, maintainability, and lifecycle management

Why do you need this guide?

This guide is essential for SAP S/4HANA customers currently running or considering a hub/standalone SAP Fiori Front-End Server deployment, and who are planning or preparing for a transition from a hub/standalone SAP Fiori Front-End Server deployment to an embedded deployment.

It provides clear, structured guidance to support a smooth transition, covering both technical and practical aspects of the move. Whether you are upgrading your SAP S/4HANA system or proactively aligning with SAP’s long term architectural direction, this guide helps you plan and execute the transition with confidence.

From planning, installation, and configuration to post migration validation and optimisation, it brings together everything you need to successfully move to an embedded SAP Fiori deployment.

It helps you:

  • Understand the benefits of moving to embedded FES deployment
  • Know the right time to make the shift
  • Navigate technical prerequisites, sizing, and system considerations
  • Simplify the migration process with actionable steps, tools, and examples
  • Ensure a seamless experience for users by resolving common issues
  • Minimise disruptions by retaining user personalisation and role assignments
  • Gain tips on how to enhance usability and performance

By following this guide, you can align with SAP best practices and unlock the full potential of SAP Fiori embedded deployment.

What's in it / What's new?

  • Simplified architecture in SAP S/4HANA 2025
    Some Front-end components are now part of the core platform, reducing system complexity
  • More practical, end-to-end guidance
    Covers not just migration steps, but also planning, validation, and long term operations
  • Better control of custom content migration
    Helps define what custom content to move and adds validation after migration instead of blindly transporting everything
  • Improved transport strategy guidance
    Real world options for handling transports depending on your landscape and ongoing development activities
  • Extended launchpad configuration coverage
    Includes transport of launchpad parameters, plug ins, and configuration settings beyond just apps and catalogs
  • Built-in validation step
    Dedicated step to validate custom content in the embedded system after migration
  • Enhanced personalisation migration
    Clear guidance using tools like /UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO to retain user settings, homepages, and UI changes
  • Explicit system alias and RFC adjustments
    Clear steps to adjust system aliases and RFC destinations post migration
  • Smarter SAP Notes handling
    Explicit step to identify and implement missing SAP Notes after migration, avoiding common post go live issues
  • Stronger focus on business roles
    Additional step to review and optimise business roles after migration in line with modern Fiori design

Becoming a SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on our  SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA topic page

Other helpful links:

  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Foundational SAP Notes for SAP S/4HANA 2025
  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates 
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: SAP User Experience in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
The SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release (fka SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) has been shipped on January 14, 2026.

With every release update, our goal is to make your interaction with the system more intuitive, efficient, and intelligent. The 2602 release is a significant step forward, focusing on consistency, AI-powered assistance, enhanced personalization, and on-the-go mobility. Let's dive into the highlights in the area of SAP User Experience!

The new shell bar is here to stay

Remember the redesigned shell bar we introduced as an opt-in feature back in 2508? Based on positive feedback and our drive for a unified experience, it is now activated by default in the 2602 release.

The new shell bar provides:

  • An updated user menu: With a larger user image and more space between items, finding what you need is easier than ever. We've also moved "Settings" to the top and separated "Sign Out" for quicker access.

     

  • Redesigned notifications: A simplified, more pleasing design with easy sorting (by date or importance) and simple grouping (Today, Yesterday, etc.) helps you stay on top of your tasks without the clutter.

Important note for a smooth transition: As this is now the default, we strongly recommend you check any test automation or RPA scripts you have, as they may need to be adapted to the new user menu structure. For this release only, you can temporarily opt-out via the "Activate New Features" switch (feature `/UI2/SHELL_BAR_TOGGLE`), but please plan to adapt your systems, as the opt-out option will not be available from the 2608 release onwards.

Direct app launch in the SAP Fiori launchpad

We've heard you! Many of our experienced users coming from the SAP GUI world missed the ability to quickly launch an application using its transaction code. I'm delighted to announce that you can now launch apps directly from the SAP Fiori launchpad search bar using the app ID or transaction code.


For power users, this restores a familiar and efficient navigation pattern, lowering the barrier for those transitioning to SAP Fiori, as they can combine new UX principles with known shortcuts.

Enhancements to My Home

My Home continues to evolve as your central, personalized starting point. In 2602, we're giving you more control and enhancing communication:

  • Required reading for news: Administrators can now mark important news articles, such as new policies or critical process changes, as "required reading." Users must acknowledge these articles, which helps ensure compliance and consistent knowledge across the organization.

  • Greater layout control: You can now choose to hide the entire "News" or "Pages" sections on your My Home screen. This allows you to reduce clutter and tailor your homepage to display only the information and tools that are most relevant to your role, boosting your personal productivity.



An intelligent assistant for your daily tasks

Artificial intelligence is at the heart of the intelligent enterprise, and we're embedding it directly into your user experience.

The 2602 release distinguishes between base and premium AI features:

  • Base features (free of charge): After signing the acceptance of SAP AI terms for base AI-assisted features in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you gain access to features like AI-assisted enterprise search, easy filter, and smart summarization.
  • Premium features (requires license): With the appropriate licenses (like SAP AI units and package Joule Premium for financal management), you can unlock advanced capabilities. In 2602, we are excited to announce the general availability (GA) of AI-assisted smart personalization of applications in My Home and AI-assisted error explanation.

Furthermore, we are continuing to beta test AI-assisted task automation, which allows users to create "smart helpers" to automate repetitive activities. This powerful tool keeps the user in the loop while dramatically improving efficiency. If you're interested in shaping the future of AI in our products, I encourage you to sign up for our beta testing program here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/4086

Fast with SAP Fiori

We’re thrilled to announce the launch of our brand-new video series, "Fast with SAP Fiori"! The first three episodes are now live and ready to accelerate your SAP Fiori journey.

What’s coming:

  • Complete 10-episode series covering essential SAP Fiori concepts
  • Soon available on YouTube for easy access
  • Multi-language support including English, German, French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Italian

Ready to dive in? Check out the first three episodes now, and stay tuned for the complete series launch!

  • Video 1 - https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/ce7RMfU?rc=10&doi=SAP1286910 
  • Video 2 - https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/Ef5HP5X?rc=10&doi=SAP1286912 
  • Video 3 - https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/558Yrrv?rc=10&doi=SAP1286922 

Productivity on the go: SAP Fiori for mobile optimization

In today's flexible work environment, being productive on your smartphone is essential. That's why we've introduced SAP Fiori elements mobile phone optimization. This delivers a mobile-first experience by providing a compact, modern, and high-performance framework for key mobile scenarios. We've simplified navigation and reduced complexity by focusing on what's essential for users on the move, allowing you to get business done anywhere, anytime. Feel free to sign up for our beta testing program here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/4090

A single front door: Introducing SAP Start (SAP-managed)

Imagine a single, unified landing page for all your SAP Cloud ERP products. That is the vision for SAP Start, and with the 2602 release, we are introducing a new SAP-managed version.

This means you can activate it directly from SAP for Me with no additional implementation effort. In its initial release, SAP Start provides seamless, single sign-on access to SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Concur, and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2. This is just the beginning, and we are eager for your feedback through our Early Adopter Care program to shape its future.

 

Your voice matters: Get involved!

Many of the features in this release, including the highly-requested direct app launch, originated as suggestions on the SAP Customer Influence Portal (CIP.) This is proof that your feedback directly shapes the future of our products.

I encourage you to explore these new features, see how they fit your business needs, and continue to share your insights with us through the SAP Community and the Customer Influence portal.

The 2602 release truly elevates the SAP User Experience, and we can't wait to see how you leverage these innovations to run your business better.

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for SAP User Experience as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

🔎 Find all our assets 

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 accelerated SAP’s pivot to AI. Most of the keynote demos were heavy on AI, including introducing new engagement layer experiences to complement the existing application experiences. The magic of the engagement layer is that it allows you to interact with your ERP system in a conversational way, starting with your intent and the system will generate an interface based on the context of your request. While this might represent a more natural way to work, it also represents a major change to how people have been working with SAP applications for the past few decades.

The new engagement layer lets you interact with SAP in a conversational way.

I had the opportunity to see many deep-dive sessions that explained the new UX and how we expect people to work with it. More importantly, I spoke with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. While Joule Work will certainly be a game changer for many, the big open question is how to get there. As with all IT transformations, it will involve a combination of changes across people, processes, and technology.

Embedded AI meets customers where they are

The first session that I presented was on “Creating better user experiences with AI-powered SAP Fiori apps ASUG.” I started out by talking about overcoming usability challenges with AI and then did a live demo of around 10 cool productivity features. What’s unique about the embedded AI capabilities in SAP Cloud ERP is that it is built into the existing interfaces that people know, so it allows them to go through their work more quickly by using AI to search, summarize, and enter data.

There are many AI-powered features that make people more productive when using SAP Cloud ERP.

The slides from the session (PUX1228) include links where you can see short recorded demos of these features. These capabilities are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Some of them are coming to private cloud in upcoming releases.

Some questions that customers asked that might be of general interest:

  • Does the engagement layer replace SAP Fiori? No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. (Thanks for the question, Tony)
  • Does the engagement layer use SAP Fiori elements? The answer here is more nuanced. We use the OData metadata that is used by Fiori elements to get the SAP S/4HANA data to render high quality cards in the engagement layer.
    So, the engagement layer is independent of the Fiori apps themselves. The engagement layer does not call the Fiori apps for the data, but it uses the same underlying data source, which are the OData services and annotations. (Great question, Kelly)

Lots of people were interested in having a better user experience with AI assistance.

Fear, optimism, and the AI Skills gap

My other presentation was a “community conversation” in the ASUG Hub. My colleague, Nicole Smythe and I facilitated the session along with Liz Tuckwell and Nadalee Williams from ASUG. The goal was to collect ideas on AI adoption from the attendees and equip them with information so they could create a 90-day plan to turn their thoughts into action and move forward with AI in your organization. After providing an overview of Joule Work, we broke into teams and people discussed barriers to implementing AI in their organizations and how they overcame them.

Top problems that the audience identified included:

  • Data. How to get the data clean so any AI agents can work properly and consistently. Most people know they need to fix their business processes and clean their data before they can benefit from AI.
  • Governance. People see the need for IT to manage this centrally. Currently, many innovative teams want to get started with AI and are doing things on their own without corporate oversight. We saw this situation a decade ago when some business managers purchased cloud services for sales or HR or other domains if they did not receive sufficient support from their IT teams.
  • Cost. How to meter AI consumption and allocate charges to different users.
  • AI Expertise. Upskilling teams on AI and its capabilities. Currently, this is a side job for most people, so they are not putting enough focus on this. Once company had a rotation program where top developers could allocate 50% of their time to learning AI topics and then bring those skills back to their teams.
  • Talent management. With fewer entry-level workers or people doing repetitive jobs, it is harder to identify the best employees, so you can focus on retention and building a leadership pipeline.

Participants discussed how to overcome barriers to AI adoption.

Other interesting sessions on AI, UX, and AI+UX

Most of the other sessions I attended were on AI, UX, or their intersection. Hearing the questions people asked and what they talked about afterwards will be helpful in refining our agenda for our next SAP Connect Day for UX, which is in Palo Alto on June 11. We’ll go deep on our UX strategy and share some new functionality, both released and unreleased. There will be two hands-on exercises, one on building applications and the other building Smart Helpers (currently in beta).

  • Katja Zoch and Sebastian Steinhauer had a session on how to extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core. They focused on what is clean core, why its important, and how to extend your core ERP system using clean core principles.  
  • Chris Hoffmann had several sessions on the show floor. They all focused on different dimensions of the AI-powered user experience in the SAP Business Suite. One was a broad survey of functionality, another focused on AI-assisted task automation, and the other talked about roadmap items. All showed how AI adoption is on the path towards human‑supervised autonomous ERP.
  • Tom Reiss presented on SAP Fiori in action, highlighting how modern UX helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. He also had a roadmap session with Christina Salwizek featuring web and mobile innovations that are on the horizon.
  • Sophia Levens had some great sessions on the new Joule user experience, focusing on both how it works and the implications for users. She also had a strategy-focused talk with SAP’s chief designer officer, Arin Bhowmick, where they explored where user engagement and enterprise UX are headed.
  • Bert Schultz and Esther Vrieze spoke about different ways to migrate to SAP S/4HANA and the relative benefits among the different approaches.
  • Maura Hameroff and Rudi Hois talked about SAP Cloud ERP Private and the many innovations that customers can expect to see in upcoming releases.

We held our SAP Fiori Development Roundtable live from the show

To add a little extra energy to our already lively discussions, I joined my co-host Sebastian Werner live from the show floor. We spent lots of time talking about Joule Studio and how it fits with our existing development tools. If you want to join the next monthly call, please register.

AI vibe from across the conference

While the sessions are a great way to learn about hot topics, the best way to hear about how customers are using our solutions and what they are planning is in the hallways, food areas, UXpresso Café (successor to the Fiori Café), happy hours, and demo pods. The questions after sessions are also revealing. This year, the customers I spoke to were concerned about:

  • How the new engagement layer (Joule Work) fits with SAP Fiori
  • How to get started with AI, especially in private cloud and on-prem environments
  • If their existing Fiori apps will continue to work (yes!) and if they should continue to build new Fiori apps (yes!)
  • All the topics listed above from the ASUG community conversation (data, governance, cost, AI skills, and talent pipeline)

Next steps

Perhaps you are still digesting all the announcements from Sapphire and figuring out their impact on your business. To help you prepare for the future, consider discussing the following questions with your team.

  1. What business problems are most pressing and can benefit from automation? How will we meter the costs, set appropriate limits, and measure the return on investment?
  2. Is our data clean, consistent across different parts of the business, ready to train the AI system, and be part of automated workflows? How much autonomy are we willing to give agents and what is the best way to supervise / manage / audit their activities?
  3. How will we integrate AI into our SAP landscape? Are we using SAP’s embedded capabilities, Joule, the new engagement layer, or external AI-powered apps running on SAP data (subject to the API policy)?
  4. How will we address change management with our business users?
  5. How will we manage AI governance, risk, and security? Using SAP’s AI simplifies the answer to this question as we cover much of this, but you still need robust AI policies in your organization

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

If you are in the Silicon Valley area or can fly there on June 11, please join us for SAP Connect Day for UX. We'll go deeper into all the UX and related AI topics listed in this post.
https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html 

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: SAP UX Update: The New Joule Work Engagement Layer Experiences
entry.content:
What is Joule Work, and what does it mean for you? What do the new Joule Work engagement layer experiences mean for existing application experiences? How can you try it out? Read on to get an overview of Joule Work and answers to these questions.

What is Joule Work?

Joule Work provides an AI-based user experience that transforms how work happens. With Joule work, people express what they want to accomplish, and Joule surfaces the right insights, automates routine work, and coordinates AI agents across systems to achieve it – all in one dynamic workspace. It acts as an engagement layer that spans across SAP products and beyond, and coexists with current experiences provided by pre-defined application UIs.

It represents a new way of interacting with business systems, since it provides:

  • One single place where a user can get tasks done, rather than the traditional app-centric interaction model, where users need to navigate to different applications or even to different products.
  • An intent-driven, situational experience, where users state their intents in natural language and the system builds on the fly accordingly, taking the current business and user context (the current situation) into account: fetching data, triggering AI agents, and visualizing the outcome appropriately.

Figure 1: Joule Work experiences: Conversations, Spaces, and Develop. Alt Text: The image shows 3 screenshots, one each for the three experiences. The top of the image has a heading  “Joule Work”, above a row with five attributes: Dynamic, Proactive, Agentic, Intent-based, Omnipresent.

Joule Work provides these experiences:

  • Conversations: this experience allows people to express intent and explore what they want to accomplish through dialogue with Joule. It is an AI-powered chat supporting role-based intents, multiple conversation threads, with type-ahead, auto-suggestions and multi-facetted types of responses (text, tables, charts etc.). Joule brings together guidance and outcomes from across SAP and third-party systems. Conversations can also act as an entry point to Spaces for more complex workflows.
  • Spaces: provides flexible AI-generated spaces that dynamically adapt content based on user intent and context, for dealing with simple as well as complex tasks and issues. Each space is composed in real time to bring the right data, tools, and context together for a specific goal, allowing people to work in an environment that adapts to their present goal, instead of navigating applications and their pre-defined UIs.​
  • Develop: this experience is provided by Joule Studio, enabling developers to vibe-code, test and deploy AI agents, applications, and automations across SAP and third-party systems. Joule Work provides a convenient entry point to Joule Studio for developers and business users alike, giving them an AI-powered user experience for pro-code and low-code tools.

To get a better idea of these experiences, have a look at Joule Work in action:

Click on the image to start the video.

Joule Work mobile app

The Joule Work mobile app provides Joule Work Conversations and Spaces for mobile phones and tablets. Figure 2 shows some examples.

Figure 2: Joule Work mobile app, examples of Conversations and Spaces. Alt Text: two pairs of iPhone images. On the left Joule Work Conversations, showing a start and a screen listing some return orders and a user prompt. On the right a screen with text “Create a new space” and a user input “Create a space to sow my open customer returns for BikePro”, and a screen showing the resulting space with a chart, some cards with recommended tasks, and a return order analysis.

Find out more here. 

  • Joule Work mobile app is here!

What are the benefits for businesses and their users?

Businesses expect that the new AI/agentic, intent-driven way of working that is taking hold across the industry is ushering in a new era of user interaction and user experience, that will bring them gains in productivity and data quality, along with better business insights and outcomes, while saving training costs and reducing user-support costs.

Joule Work from SAP has an ingrained understanding of the customer’s business processes: hence it is best placed to deliver on the above promise. It can provide experiences that bring together the right data and services from across our applications to guide users from initial intent to a successful outcome.

It helps users get their work done faster, more easily, and with better outcomes, by:

  • Providing one place to get their work done, so they don’t have to navigate to many different applications and products, and don’t have to navigate through layers of application UIs, thus reducing the number of clicks and interactions significantly.
  • Allowing users to state their intent in natural language with a conversational interface, then triggering the relevant actions to achieve their intent, and showing the outcome(s) in a personalized way, tailored to the user’s needs.

Which target groups of users and use cases is Joule Work meant for?

Business and technical users. The goal is to help all our customer’s SAP users get their work done more easily, focusing on outcomes, so it is not limited to specific target groups.

The Joule Work Develop experience is provided by Joule Studio. Find out more about the new version of Joule Studio in this news article:

  • Announcing New Joule Studio for Enterprise Scale Agentic Development.

What does this mean for existing UIs and SAP Fiori apps?

Existing SAP products and applications built with SAP’s UI technology stack, as well as other apps in our portfolio, will continue to progress with their roadmap and planned product strategy. Joule Work has no impact on existing UIs, instead it complements them. Customers will continue to get the benefits from existing applications and their investment is protected.

Joule Work provides an additional option for users to interact with the system. Wherever they find that it helps them get tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes, they are likely to do those tasks with Joule Work. Wherever they feel that their familiar, pre-defined applications are better suited to a task, they will continue using them.

This means that SAP delivers two kinds of experiences:

  • Application experiences (from SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Analytics Cloud, etc.): SAP and customer-built transactional and analytical application experiences.
  • Engagement layer experiences (Joule Work experiences): AI-native, agentic, and generative experiences that help users interact with and orchestrate work across those applications.

Our users are used to application UIs and pre-defined navigation paths, how to manage the transition?

The existing product home pages and cross-product entry points such as SAP Build Work Zone and Joule Work mobile app (formerly called SAP Mobile Start) continue to be available for providing pre-defined as well as customer-defined pages and navigation menus. Similarly, the existing applications continue to be available, with their application experiences.

Users working in the web browser can open Joule Work from Joule and hence start getting familiar with the new way of working. Since the new experience co-exists with the current experience it will be easy for users to adapt and adopt. We expect the transition to be user-led, in that they will choose whichever experience helps them get specific tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes.

If users are already familiar with popular consumer applications such as ChatGPT, Google Gemini or Claude Sonnet, then they will be able to build on this to leverage the next generation user experience provided by Joule Work, with the new ways of interacting.

Does it complement or does it replace existing solutions such as SAP Start, SAP Build Work Zone, and SAP Mobile Start?

It complements them. The existing solutions allow customer administrators to pre-define layouts, content and navigation options, which helps users access applications and information using familiar pages, menus and links. It makes it easy for them to understand the site and feel comfortable using it in their daily work.
